content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the co-occurrence of other resistance markers, such as ESBL genes (e.g., blaCTX-M), facilitates plasmid maintenance or transfer efficiency through co-selection.

content_rubrics.title: Surveillance and Diagnostic Gaps
content_rubrics.description: The answer should highlight that inadequate surveillance or the lack of rapid molecular diagnostics can delay the detection of emerging resistance genes (e.g., blaVEB-25), facilitating undetected spread.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of M1 vs. M2 Polarization
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that M2-polarized TAMs promote immunosuppression and resistance to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, whereas M1-polarized TAMs (or repolarization to M1) enhance responsiveness and therapeutic efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: Superior Safety Profile of Lecanemab
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that lecanemab has a lower incidence or risk of Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities (ARIA) compared to aducanumab.

content_rubrics.title: Differential Efficacy Across Cognitive Scales
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention findings from meta-analyses (e.g., PMID 38429615) indicating that while aducanumab showed greater statistical improvements in CDR-SB or MMSE, lecanemab showed better preservation of Activities of Daily Living (ADCS-ADL).

content_rubrics.title: General Efficacy in Slowing Decline
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that both drugs are effective in lowering amyloid burden and slowing clinical decline (generally cited around 27-30% relative slowing for lecanemab) compared to placebo.

content_rubrics.title: Specific ARIA Subtypes (ARIA-E and ARIA-H)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the safety comparison involves both ARIA-E (edema/effusion) and ARIA-H (hemorrhage/microhemorrhage), with lecanemab generally showing lower rates of these specific events.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of APOE4 Status
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that APOE4 carrier status (especially homozygosity) increases the risk of ARIA for these anti-amyloid therapies.

content_rubrics.title: Current Availability/Approval Context
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the difference in status, such as lecanemab having full approval or being the current standard, whereas aducanumab has been discontinued or had limited uptake due to safety/efficacy concerns.

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy Evidence Hierarchy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that VMAT2 inhibitors (e.g., tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, valbenazine) have robust evidence from randomized controlled trials and/or FDA approval for treating HD chorea, whereas antipsychotics are often used off-label with less robust RCT evidence.

content_rubrics.title: Depression and Suicidality Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that Tetrabenazine is associated with a risk of depression and suicidality (boxed warning), while newer VMAT2 inhibitors (Valbenazine) or antipsychotics may have more favorable mood profiles or be preferred in patients with psychiatric comorbidities.

content_rubrics.title: Parkinsonism and EPS Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that antipsychotics (dopamine receptor blockers) generally carry a higher risk of inducing parkinsonism or rigidity compared to VMAT2 inhibitors, or that VMAT2 inhibitors are associated with lower rates of these motor side effects.

content_rubrics.title: QT Prolongation Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish that while Tetrabenazine and many antipsychotics carry risks for QT prolongation, Valbenazine has been shown to have no clinically significant effect on the QT interval.

content_rubrics.title: Role of Antipsychotics
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that antipsychotics are particularly useful or indicated when patients have comorbid psychiatric symptoms (e.g., psychosis, irritability) alongside chorea.

content_rubrics.title: Association with Increased Mortality

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the prognostic outcome is heavily influenced by the severity of illness (e.g., septic shock, ICU admission, high Pitt bacteremia score) and patient comorbidities, which can be independent predictors of mortality alongside or confounding the delay in therapy.

content_rubrics.title: Empiric vs. Definitive Therapy Nuance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss the nuance that inappropriate *empirical* therapy may not always result in higher mortality if appropriate *definitive* therapy is administered once susceptibility results are known (referencing findings like Kang et al., 2004, or mixed evidence).

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that ESBL-producing infections are associated with a higher frequency of inappropriate initial therapy compared to non-ESBL infections, which contributes to the observed mortality differences.

content_rubrics.title: Quantification of Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide quantitative evidence (e.g., Odds Ratios, Relative Risks, or percentage comparisons) from studies to illustrate the magnitude of the mortality risk associated with delayed therapy or ESBL status.

content_rubrics.title: Predictive value for B-cell depletion
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that ACPA and/or RF positivity predicts a better clinical response (e.g., higher remission, lower discontinuation) to B-cell depletion therapy (rituximab).

content_rubrics.title: Predictive value for IL-6 blockade
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that ACPA and RF status are weak or non-significant predictors for response to IL-6 receptor blockade (tocilizumab), with similar efficacy observed across serostatus groups.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative predictive strength
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly compare the two therapies, noting that serostatus is a significantly stronger predictor of response for rituximab than for tocilizumab.

content_rubrics.title: Outcomes for seronegative patients
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that seronegative patients tend to have poorer outcomes with rituximab compared to seropositive patients, whereas their response to IL-6 inhibitors is generally comparable to seropositive patients.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical implication for treatment selection
content_rubrics.description: The answer should suggest that seropositivity supports the prioritization of B-cell depletion therapy, whereas seronegativity may suggest alternative therapies (or that rituximab is less preferred).

content_rubrics.title: Superior LDL-C Reduction Magnitude

content_rubrics.title: Reference to LAPLACE-2 Trial
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the LAPLACE-2 trial (or PMID 24825642) as key evidence demonstrating the superior LDL-C lowering efficacy of adding a PCSK9 inhibitor versus ezetimibe.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison of MACE Reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that PCSK9 inhibitors generally offer greater reduction in Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE) compared to ezetimibe, supported by network meta-analyses or comparison of landmark trial data.

content_rubrics.title: Key Cardiovascular Outcome Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference landmark cardiovascular outcome trials to contextualize the benefits, specifically FOURIER (for PCSK9 inhibitors/evolocumab) and IMPROVE-IT (for ezetimibe).

content_rubrics.title: Specific Event Reduction (MI/Stroke)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the cardiovascular benefit includes reductions in specific events such as myocardial infarction and stroke.

content_rubrics.title: Role of B2M in HLA Stability
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Beta-2 microglobulin (B2M) is essential for the proper folding, stability, and transport of HLA/MHC Class I molecules to the tumor cell surface.

content_rubrics.title: Disruption of Antigen Presentation
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that loss-of-function alterations in these pathways prevent the display of tumor antigens (or neoantigens) to the immune system.

content_rubrics.title: Evasion of CD8+ T Cell Recognition
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe how the lack of surface MHC Class I molecules renders tumor cells 'invisible' to cytotoxic CD8+ T cells, preventing T Cell Receptor (TCR) engagement.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Checkpoint Blockade Failure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should clarify that PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors function by releasing brakes on existing immune responses; without the primary antigen recognition signal (Signal 1), removing the checkpoint (Signal 2/inhibitory) is ineffective.

content_rubrics.title: Clonal Selection / Immune Pressure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that these alterations often lead to acquired resistance via the selective survival and expansion of antigen-loss variants under the immune pressure exerted by the therapy.

content_rubrics.title: Association with IFN-gamma Signaling
content_rubrics.description: The answer should ideally mention the relationship with Interferon-gamma (IFN-y) signaling (e.g., JAK1/JAK2 mutations), as IFN-y is required to upregulate MHC Class I, and defects here are a closely related resistance mechanism.

content_rubrics.description: Identifies Metformin and Insulin (specifically Glargine) as core components of evidence-based induction regimens cited in remission literature.

content_rubrics.title: Persistence and Expansion Kinetics
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the general consensus that CD28 domains typically drive rapid initial expansion but shorter persistence, whereas 4-1BB domains promote slower expansion but sustained persistence and memory T-cell formation.

content_rubrics.title: Toxicity Profile (CRS & Neurotoxicity)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that CD28-based CAR-T products are generally associated with higher rates and severity of Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity compared to 4-1BB-based products, which tend to have a safer toxicity profile.

content_rubrics.title: Relapse and Antigen Density Sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that 4-1BB domains may offer advantages in preventing relapse due to reduced exhaustion and better functionality against tumors with low antigen density compared to CD28 domains.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanistic Signaling Pathways
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain the biological mechanisms driving these differences, such as 4-1BB activating NF-κB and upregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (e.g., Bcl-xL), versus CD28 driving intense glycolytic metabolism.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical Product Examples
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite specific approved products to illustrate these phenotypes, such as Axicabtagene ciloleucel (CD28) for high expansion/toxicity versus Tisagenlecleucel (4-1BB) for persistence/lower toxicity.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that DOACs demonstrate non-inferior or comparable efficacy to Warfarin in preventing ischemic stroke and systemic embolism in older adults.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the efficacy of DOACs (stroke prevention) remains consistent across age subgroups (e.g., no significant interaction with age).

content_rubrics.title: Dual Efficacy Confirmation
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that both SGLT2 inhibitors and finerenone have been proven to slow eGFR decline and reduce the risk of progression to kidney failure in patients with T2D and CKD.

content_rubrics.title: Citation of Landmark Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference key dedicated renal outcome trials: CREDENCE and/or DAPA-CKD for SGLT2 inhibitors, and FIDELIO-DKD for finerenone.

content_rubrics.title: Lack of Direct Comparisons
content_rubrics.description: The answer should clarify that there are no direct head-to-head clinical trials comparing hard renal outcomes (e.g., kidney failure, renal death) between the two drug classes.

content_rubrics.title: Distinct Mechanisms of Action
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain the different mechanisms: SGLT2 inhibitors primarily act via hemodynamic effects (tubuloglomerular feedback) and glucose excretion, while finerenone acts via mineralocorticoid receptor antagonism (reducing inflammation and fibrosis).

content_rubrics.title: Combination Therapy (CONFIDENCE Trial)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that combination therapy (SGLT2i + finerenone) has been studied (e.g., CONFIDENCE trial) and demonstrates greater reduction in albuminuria (UACR) compared to monotherapy.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical Positioning or Indirect Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide context on their usage, such as SGLT2 inhibitors being first-line standard of care, or that indirect analyses suggest comparable risk reduction when trial definitions are aligned.

content_rubrics.title: Neurofilament Levels (NfL) as Predictor
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify Neurofilament Light Chain (NfL) or phosphorylated Neurofilament Heavy Chain (pNFH) in serum/plasma or CSF as a primary biomarker where elevated levels at diagnosis predict rapid functional decline and shorter survival.

content_rubrics.title: Genetic Mutation Status Influence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that genetic status (e.g., C9orf72 repeat expansions vs. SOD1 mutations) impacts progression, noting that C9orf72 is often associated with faster progression or higher neurofilament levels compared to SOD1.

content_rubrics.title: Site of Onset (Bulbar vs. Spinal)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should recognize site of onset as a predictor, specifically that bulbar onset is generally associated with a faster rate of progression, shorter survival, or higher biomarker levels compared to spinal onset.

content_rubrics.title: Baseline Progression Rate (ΔFS)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the pre-diagnostic or baseline rate of disease progression (often measured as the change in ALSFRS-R score over time, ΔFS) as a strong predictor of future decline.

content_rubrics.title: Respiratory Function Measures (FVC/SVC)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should list respiratory metrics measured at diagnosis, such as Forced Vital Capacity (FVC) or Slow Vital Capacity (SVC), as key clinical predictors of disease progression and survival.

content_rubrics.title: Nutritional Status / BMI
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention Body Mass Index (BMI) or nutritional status at diagnosis, specifically that lower BMI (e.g., <25) is linked to accelerated functional decline.

question: In Huntington's disease, how do VMAT2 inhibitors compare with antipsychotics in reducing chorea severity and what are the relative risks of depression, parkinsonism, and QT prolongation?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy Evidence Hierarchy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that VMAT2 inhibitors (e.g., tetrabenazine, deutetrabenazine, valbenazine) have robust evidence from randomized controlled trials and/or FDA approval for treating HD chorea, whereas antipsychotics are often used off-label with less robust RCT evidence.

content_rubrics.title: Depression and Suicidality Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that Tetrabenazine is associated with a risk of depression and suicidality (boxed warning), while newer VMAT2 inhibitors (Valbenazine) or antipsychotics may have more favorable mood profiles or be preferred in patients with psychiatric comorbidities.

content_rubrics.title: Parkinsonism and EPS Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that antipsychotics (dopamine receptor blockers) generally carry a higher risk of inducing parkinsonism or rigidity compared to VMAT2 inhibitors, or that VMAT2 inhibitors are associated with lower rates of these motor side effects.

content_rubrics.title: Genetic Loss-of-Function in Mouse Models
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that TREM2 deficiency (knockout) or risk variants (e.g., R47H) in amyloid mouse models (e.g., 5xFAD, LOAD2) lead to exacerbated amyloid pathology, reduced microglial clustering around plaques, and increased synaptic loss or neurite dystrophy.

content_rubrics.title: Human sTREM2 and Synaptic Biomarkers
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that elevated levels of soluble TREM2 (sTREM2) in human cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) correlate with biomarkers of synaptic injury, specifically 14-3-3 proteins (14-3-3ζ/δ), often noted in early disease stages.

content_rubrics.title: Galectin-3 Interaction Mechanism
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Galectin-3 (Gal3) acts as a ligand for TREM2, driving pro-inflammatory signaling; deleting Gal3 or disrupting this interaction reduces amyloid-associated inflammation and synaptic defects.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite evidence that increasing TREM2 signaling via agonists or antibodies (e.g., AL002/AL002c) promotes microglial clustering/plaque compaction and reduces neurite dystrophy.

content_rubrics.title: Microglial Barrier Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the mechanism where TREM2 enables microglia to compact plaques or form a barrier (DAM phenotype), which protects surrounding neurites and synapses from toxicity.

content_rubrics.title: Inverse Prediction of Onset
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that CAG repeat length is a strong, robust, or inverse predictor of the age of motor onset (longer repeats are associated with earlier onset).

content_rubrics.title: Prediction of Progression Rate
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that CAG repeat length predicts the rate of motor, cognitive, and functional decline, with longer repeats generally associated with more rapid clinical progression.

content_rubrics.title: Shared Determinants of Onset and Progression
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that a significant proportion (approx. two-thirds) of the factors influencing progression rates overlap with those determining age of onset (indicating shared CAG-dependent mechanisms).

content_rubrics.title: Limitations in Predicting Survival/Duration
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that CAG repeat length is a poor predictor of post-onset disease duration (survival) or specific non-motor features like weight loss.

content_rubrics.title: Necessity of Multimodal Models
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that observational data suggest integrating CAG length with other biomarkers (e.g., imaging like striatal volume, or clinical motor scores) significantly improves prognostic accuracy compared to CAG alone.

content_rubrics.title: Absence of Head-to-Head Trials

content_rubrics.title: AAV Visual Function Outcomes (RPE65)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe AAV outcomes, particularly for RPE65-associated disease, noting significant improvements in multi-luminance mobility testing (MLMT) and/or full-field light sensitivity (FST).

content_rubrics.title: CRISPR Visual Function Outcomes (CEP290)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the Phase 1/2 results for CRISPR (e.g., EDIT-101 for CEP290), noting meaningful improvements in visual outcomes (such as FST or visual acuity) in a subset of treated participants.

content_rubrics.title: Shared Procedural Safety Risks
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that both therapies carry adverse event risks associated with the subretinal delivery procedure, specifically mentioning transient intraocular inflammation or retinal detachment.

content_rubrics.title: Disparity in Evidence Maturity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish the level of evidence, noting that AAV has long-term data (e.g., Phase 3, 4-year durability) while CRISPR is currently limited to early-phase (Phase 1/2) safety and efficacy data.

content_rubrics.title: ERCC1 Expression and Platinum Resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that high ERCC1 expression is generally associated with resistance to platinum chemotherapy, while low expression correlates with sensitivity, due to ERCC1's role in the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway removing platinum-DNA adducts.

content_rubrics.title: HRD and Platinum Sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD), often caused by BRCA1/2 mutations or downregulation, leads to platinum sensitivity because tumor cells cannot efficiently repair platinum-induced double-strand breaks.

content_rubrics.title: Specific Tumor Examples (NSCLC, Ovarian, HNSCC)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide specific examples of tumor types where these relationships are observed, such as Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC), Ovarian Cancer, or Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSCC).

content_rubrics.title: Mechanisms of Acquired Resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention mechanisms by which tumors acquire resistance, such as the restoration of Homologous Recombination (HR) activity (e.g., via secondary mutations) or the upregulation of repair proteins like ERCC1.

content_rubrics.title: Predictive Value of DNA Repair Markers
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that profiling DNA repair markers (e.g., ERCC1 levels, BRCA status) is clinically relevant for predicting patient response to platinum-based therapies.

content_rubrics.title: Inpatient Video-EEG as Gold Standard
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that inpatient video-EEG (VEM) is traditionally considered the gold standard for differentiating ES from PNES due to the ability to correlate video with EEG, perform medication withdrawal, and utilize seizure provocation protocols.

content_rubrics.title: Diagnostic Yield of Prolonged Ambulatory EEG
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite recent evidence (specifically Li et al., 2024) indicating that prolonged ambulatory EEG captures diagnostic events in approximately 41-49% of recordings, making it a viable alternative.

content_rubrics.title: Benefit of Extended Monitoring Duration
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the diagnostic yield of ambulatory EEG increases with duration, noting that a significant percentage of events (e.g., ~14%) are captured during extended monitoring periods (e.g., days 5-8) that exceed typical inpatient stays.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical Question Resolution Rate
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that when diagnostic events are captured on ambulatory EEG, the specific clinical question (differentiating ES from PNES) is resolved in a high percentage of cases (approximately 85%).

content_rubrics.title: Inpatient Monitoring Limitations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that inpatient monitoring can result in inconclusive findings (e.g., ~40-42%) if events do not occur spontaneously within the admission timeframe and provocation is unsuccessful.

content_rubrics.title: Methodological Trade-offs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the trade-offs: Ambulatory EEG offers longer monitoring in a natural environment (better for infrequent spontaneous events), while Inpatient VEM offers a controlled environment with real-time observation and safety for interventions.

content_rubrics.title: ERCC1 Expression and Platinum Resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that high ERCC1 expression is generally associated with resistance to platinum chemotherapy, while low expression correlates with sensitivity, due to ERCC1's role in the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway removing platinum-DNA adducts.

content_rubrics.title: HRD and Platinum Sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD), often caused by BRCA1/2 mutations or downregulation, leads to platinum sensitivity because tumor cells cannot efficiently repair platinum-induced double-strand breaks.

content_rubrics.title: Specific Tumor Examples (NSCLC, Ovarian, HNSCC)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide specific examples of tumor types where these relationships are observed, such as Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC), Ovarian Cancer, or Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma (HNSCC).

content_rubrics.title: Mechanisms of Acquired Resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention mechanisms by which tumors acquire resistance, such as the restoration of Homologous Recombination (HR) activity (e.g., via secondary mutations) or the upregulation of repair proteins like ERCC1.

content_rubrics.title: Predictive Value of DNA Repair Markers
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that profiling DNA repair markers (e.g., ERCC1 levels, BRCA status) is clinically relevant for predicting patient response to platinum-based therapies.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: RB1 Loss
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify RB1 loss as a primary mechanism of resistance. It should explain that loss of the Retinoblastoma protein removes the substrate for CDK4/6, rendering the inhibitors ineffective as the cell cycle progresses independently of this checkpoint.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: CCNE1 Amplification
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify CCNE1 (Cyclin E1) amplification or overexpression as a key resistance mechanism. It must explain that this drives the cell cycle via CDK2 activation, effectively bypassing the inhibition of CDK4/6.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: ESR1 Mutations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention ESR1 mutations (e.g., Y537S, D538G) as a clinically relevant mechanism of acquired resistance, leading to ligand-independent ER signaling.

content_rubrics.title: Strategy: Targeting CDK2
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss targeting CDK2 (e.g., with specific inhibitors) as a potential strategy to overcome resistance driven by CCNE1 amplification or RB1 loss.

content_rubrics.title: Strategy: PI3K/AKT/mTOR Inhibition
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the use of PI3K, AKT, or mTOR inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, everolimus, capivasertib) in combination regimens to target compensatory signaling pathways associated with resistance.

content_rubrics.title: Strategy: Next-Generation SERDs/PROTACs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should recommend the use of next-generation Selective Estrogen Receptor Degraders (SERDs) like camizestrant or elacestrant, or PROTACs, particularly to address ESR1-mutated or endocrine-resistant disease.

content_rubrics.title: Sustained Multi-Year FVIII Activity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that AAV gene therapy achieves sustained Factor VIII expression levels (e.g., mild to normal range) that persist over multi-year follow-up (e.g., 2 to 5 years).

content_rubrics.description: The answer must compare the treatment burden, highlighting AAV as a one-time intervention versus the lifelong, frequent intravenous infusions required for standard prophylactic recombinant factor replacement.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that while AAV therapy is sustained, there can be variability in durability, with some patients experiencing a decline in FVIII activity over time (e.g., due to immune response or hepatocyte turnover), unlike the predictable pharmacokinetics of replacement therapy.

content_rubrics.title: CAG Repeat Length Impact
content_rubrics.description: States that CAG repeat length is the primary inverse determinant of age of onset, explaining approximately 40-73% of the variance.

content_rubrics.title: TCERG1 as a Modifier
content_rubrics.description: Identifies TCERG1 (specifically tandem hexamer repeats) as a genetic modifier, noting it explains approximately 1.4-2.1% of the variance.

content_rubrics.title: MC1R as a Modifier
content_rubrics.description: Identifies MC1R (specifically the p.R151C variant) as a genetic modifier, explaining approximately 1.4% of the residual variance.

content_rubrics.title: DNA Repair Pathway Involvement
content_rubrics.description: Mentions DNA repair genes (such as FAN1, PMS2, MSH3, or GPR161) as significant contributors to the variance in age of onset.

content_rubrics.title: Cumulative Variance of Modifiers
content_rubrics.description: Provides an estimate of the additional variance explained by genetic modifiers (e.g., ~10-20% of total variance or ~8-13% of residual variance).

content_rubrics.title: Other Pathway Modifiers
content_rubrics.description: Mentions involvement of other pathways such as glutamate signaling (GRIN2A/B), apoptosis (ASK1, MAP2K6), or ubiquitin (UCH-L1) in modifying age of onset.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Action Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish that AAV micro-dystrophin involves gene replacement with a truncated dystrophin construct, whereas exon-skipping approaches (AAV-mediated or ASOs) modulate pre-mRNA splicing to restore the reading frame and produce a shortened functional protein.

content_rubrics.title: Micro-dystrophin Clinical Functional Outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer must mention that in the Phase 3 EMBARK trial (or recent clinical updates), systemic AAV micro-dystrophin (delandistrogene moxeparvovec) demonstrated robust micro-dystrophin expression but failed to meet the primary functional endpoint (NSAA) at 52 weeks.

content_rubrics.title: Exon-Skipping Efficacy Evidence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference efficacy data for exon-skipping, specifically citing the robust functional improvements and dose-dependent expression seen in canine models using AAV-mediated skipping (Le Guiner et al., 2014) OR contrasting the modest efficacy of current clinical ASOs.

content_rubrics.title: Cardiopulmonary Outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that preclinical studies (murine or canine) indicate AAV-mediated therapies (micro-dystrophin or skipping) can attenuate dystrophic cardiomyopathy or improve hemodynamic parameters, though clinical cardiac data remains less definitive compared to skeletal muscle endpoints.

content_rubrics.title: Safety and Dosing Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer should highlight safety differences, noting that systemic AAV gene transfer is a single-dose therapy associated with significant immune-mediated risks (e.g., liver injury, myositis, SAEs), whereas ASO exon-skipping requires repeated dosing with a generally better safety profile.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Action (Receptor Selectivity)
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that Pimavanserin selectively targets 5-HT2A receptors (inverse agonist/antagonist) and, crucially, does not block Dopamine D2 receptors, unlike most atypical antipsychotics.

content_rubrics.title: Preservation of Motor Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that Pimavanserin does not worsen motor symptoms or parkinsonism, distinguishing it from atypical antipsychotics (like clozapine or quetiapine) which may carry risks due to D2 antagonism.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative Efficacy vs. Clozapine
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that while Pimavanserin is effective against placebo, Clozapine is generally considered to have superior or robust efficacy, though it is limited by its safety profile.

content_rubrics.title: Quetiapine Evidence Limitation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that Quetiapine, while widely used off-label, lacks robust evidence of efficacy in clinical trials for PDP.

content_rubrics.title: Mortality and Safety Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer must mention that Pimavanserin is associated with a lower mortality risk (specifically citing around 35% lower mortality) compared to other atypical antipsychotics.

content_rubrics.title: Monitoring Requirements
content_rubrics.description: The answer should highlight that Pimavanserin does not require the rigorous blood monitoring (for agranulocytosis) that is mandatory for Clozapine.

all_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Action (Receptor Selectivity)
all_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that Pimavanserin selectively targets 5-HT2A receptors (inverse agonist/antagonist) and, crucially, does not block Dopamine D2 receptors, unlike most atypical antipsychotics.

all_rubrics.title: Preservation of Motor Function
all_rubrics.description: The answer must state that Pimavanserin does not worsen motor symptoms or parkinsonism, distinguishing it from atypical antipsychotics (like clozapine or quetiapine) which may carry risks due to D2 antagonism.

all_rubrics.title: Comparative Efficacy vs. Clozapine
all_rubrics.description: The answer should note that while Pimavanserin is effective against placebo, Clozapine is generally considered to have superior or robust efficacy, though it is limited by its safety profile.

all_rubrics.title: Quetiapine Evidence Limitation
all_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that Quetiapine, while widely used off-label, lacks robust evidence of efficacy in clinical trials for PDP.

all_rubrics.title: Mortality and Safety Profile
all_rubrics.description: The answer must mention that Pimavanserin is associated with a lower mortality risk (specifically citing around 35% lower mortality) compared to other atypical antipsychotics.

all_rubrics.title: Monitoring Requirements
all_rubrics.description: The answer should highlight that Pimavanserin does not require the rigorous blood monitoring (for agranulocytosis) that is mandatory for Clozapine.

content_rubrics.title: Pathway: DSB-induced DDR via ATM/ATR
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that CRISPR-Cas9 induced double-strand breaks (DSBs) activate the DNA damage response (DDR), specifically involving the kinases ATM and/or ATR which phosphorylate and stabilize p53.

content_rubrics.title: Downstream Mechanism: p21 and Arrest/Apoptosis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that activated p53 upregulates p21 (CDKN1A), which functions to induce cell cycle arrest (G1/S or G2/M) or leads to apoptosis in hPSCs.

content_rubrics.title: Impact on Editing Efficiency (HDR Barrier)
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that p53 activation acts as a barrier to gene editing, specifically reducing the efficiency of homology-directed repair (HDR) because cells attempting repair are often eliminated or arrested.

content_rubrics.title: Impact on Proliferation (Toxicity)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe how p53 activation negatively affects proliferation by causing significant cell death (toxicity) or senescence, thereby reducing the yield of edited cells.

content_rubrics.title: Risk of Selecting p53 Mutants
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that because wild-type p53 induces toxicity in edited cells, the process creates a selection pressure that favors the survival of cells with p53 mutations, posing a risk for genomic instability.

content_rubrics.title: High-Fidelity Cas9: Binding vs. Cleavage Specificity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that high-fidelity Cas9 variants (e.g., Cas9-HF1, evoCas9) primarily enhance cleavage specificity rather than binding specificity. They require stricter complementarity to catalyze DNA cutting, even if they still bind to off-target sites with affinities similar to wild-type SpCas9.

content_rubrics.title: High-Fidelity Cas9: Conformational/Energetic Checkpoints
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the molecular mechanism of high-fidelity variants as raising the energy barrier for the transition to the catalytically active state (often involving the HNH nuclease domain or REC domains). This ensures that mismatches destabilize the active conformation, preventing cleavage.

content_rubrics.title: Cas12a: PAM Recognition Differences
content_rubrics.description: The answer must identify that Cas12a recognizes T-rich PAM sequences (e.g., TTTV), contrasting with SpCas9's G-rich (NGG) PAM. This difference restricts the genomic search space and influences the potential off-target landscape.

content_rubrics.title: SpCas9: Structural Basis of Mismatch Tolerance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that wild-type SpCas9 possesses conformational flexibility (particularly in the HNH domain) that allows it to transition to an active cleavage state even when the guide RNA and target DNA are not perfectly matched.

content_rubrics.title: Cas12a: Structural Specificity Features
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention structural features contributing to Cas12a's fidelity, such as a longer guide RNA-DNA heteroduplex, specific structural clamps (e.g., REC3), or distinct R-loop formation dynamics compared to Cas9.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanistic Distinction (Donor/DSBs)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that HDR relies on double-strand breaks (DSBs) and requires an exogenous donor template, whereas Base Editing (or Prime Editing) corrects mutations without generating DSBs or requiring a donor template.

content_rubrics.title: Efficiency in Post-Mitotic vs. Dividing Cells
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that HDR efficiency is significantly lower in post-mitotic (non-dividing) retinal cells due to low homologous recombination activity, whereas Base Editing remains effective in these cells. HDR is noted as effective in proliferating iPSCs.

content_rubrics.title: Specific Base Editing Example (KCNJ13)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the specific example of Adenine Base Editors (e.g., ABE8e) correcting the *KCNJ13* mutation in patient-derived iPSC-RPE cells (or similar specific in vitro evidence).

content_rubrics.title: Specific HDR Application in iPSCs

content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that high ERCC1 expression is generally associated with resistance to platinum chemotherapy, while low expression correlates with sensitivity, due to ERCC1's role in the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway removing platinum-DNA adducts.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD), often caused by BRCA1/2 mutations or downregulation, leads to platinum sensitivity because tumor cells cannot efficiently repair platinum-induced double-strand breaks.

content_rubrics.title: Stroke Prevention in CKD Stages 3-4
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in patients with mild-to-moderate CKD (Stages 3-4), DOACs demonstrate comparable or superior efficacy to warfarin for stroke prevention.

content_rubrics.title: Major Bleeding Risk in CKD Stages 3-4
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that DOACs are associated with a reduced risk of major bleeding compared to warfarin in patients with CKD Stages 3-4.

content_rubrics.title: Outcomes in Dialysis/End-Stage Renal Disease
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that evidence for DOACs in dialysis-dependent patients (Stage 5D) is limited or inconclusive, generally showing similar efficacy to warfarin without the clear safety benefits seen in earlier stages.

content_rubrics.title: Renal Function Preservation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that DOACs are associated with better renal outcomes, such as a lower risk of acute kidney injury (AKI) or slower progression of CKD, compared to warfarin.

content_rubrics.title: Specific Consideration of Apixaban
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify Apixaban as a preferred agent or one with specific evidence supporting its safety (particularly lower GI bleeding) and efficacy in the CKD population.

content_rubrics.title: Comparable Overall Efficacy (ALMS Trial)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in major multinational trials (specifically Appel et al., 2009), MMF and Cyclophosphamide demonstrated similar rates of complete renal response (approximately 56% vs 53%) with no significant difference in the overall population.

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy in Asian Populations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss evidence (e.g., meta-analyses like Zhang et al. or Jiang et al.) suggesting that MMF may have superior efficacy or higher remission rates specifically in Asian or Chinese patient populations compared to Cyclophosphamide.

content_rubrics.title: Gonadal Toxicity and Fertility
content_rubrics.description: The answer must highlight that Cyclophosphamide carries a significantly higher risk of ovarian failure, amenorrhea, or infertility, making MMF the preferred option for patients concerned about fertility preservation.

content_rubrics.title: Infection Risk Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that MMF is generally associated with a lower risk of infections (or severe infections) compared to Cyclophosphamide.

content_rubrics.title: Alopecia and Leukopenia
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that Cyclophosphamide is associated with higher rates of alopecia (hair loss) and potentially leukopenia compared to MMF.

content_rubrics.title: Multitarget Therapy Context
content_rubrics.description: The answer may mention that a multitarget regimen (MMF + Tacrolimus) has shown superiority to Cyclophosphamide in achieving complete remission in some studies (e.g., Liu et al., 2015), distinguishing this from MMF monotherapy.

content_rubrics.title: Prion-like Transneuronal Spread
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe how pathological tau spreads between neurons in a prion-like or seed-competent manner, utilizing mechanisms like synaptic connections or receptor-mediated endocytosis to propagate pathology across neural networks.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanisms of Synaptic Dysfunction
content_rubrics.description: The answer must detail specific mechanisms by which tau impairs synapses, such as the disruption of Long-Term Potentiation (LTP), loss of dendritic spines, microtubule instability, or interference with neurotransmitter receptors (e.g., NMDARs).

content_rubrics.title: Role of Toxic Tau Species
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that soluble, oligomeric, or hyperphosphorylated forms of tau (seed-competent species) are the primary drivers of early synaptic toxicity, distinct from or preceding the formation of insoluble fibrillar tangles.

content_rubrics.title: Synergy with Amyloid-β (Aβ)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the interaction between Aβ and tau, explaining that Aβ often initiates or amplifies tau misfolding/spreading, or that both proteins work synergistically to drive synaptic damage.

content_rubrics.title: Correlation with Memory Impairment
content_rubrics.description: The answer must link the described cellular mechanisms to functional outcomes, noting that synaptic dysfunction correlates with specific memory deficits (e.g., spatial learning) or cognitive decline in AD models or human progression.

content_rubrics.title: Integration of Human and Model Evidence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite evidence from both experimental models (e.g., transgenic mice, seeded primary neurons) and human studies (e.g., postmortem analysis of seeding activity in regions like the ITG) to support the claims.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: Loss of Nuclear Function & Cryptic Exons
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that TDP-43 aggregation leads to its nuclear depletion, resulting in the loss of repressor function and the inclusion of nonconserved cryptic exons during RNA splicing.

content_rubrics.title: Key Molecular Targets: STMN2 or UNC13A
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify specific gene targets affected by TDP-43 dysfunction, such as STMN2 (Stathmin-2) or UNC13A, which are critical for neuronal function and repair.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: Cytoplasmic Toxic Gain-of-Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe cytoplasmic toxicity mechanisms, such as mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, impairment of transport, or sequestration of other proteins/RNAs.

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic Strategy: Ataxin-2 Reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the use of Antisense Oligonucleotides (ASOs) targeting Ataxin-2 to reduce TDP-43 aggregation and extend survival, a strategy with strong preclinical evidence.

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic Strategy: Splicing Correction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss therapeutic approaches aimed at correcting splicing defects (e.g., restoring STMN2 expression) using ASOs or other genetic tools.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: Prion-like Propagation or Phase Separation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) or the prion-like propagation of TDP-43 aggregates as a mechanism for disease spread and pathology.

content_rubrics.title: Clearance Mechanisms: Autophagy/Proteasome
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that impairment of protein clearance pathways (autophagy or ubiquitin-proteasome system) contributes to aggregation, and enhancing these is a potential therapeutic strategy.

content_rubrics.title: Negative Impact of Antibiotics on Clinical Outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that antibiotic exposure, particularly prior to or during the early phase of immunotherapy, is associated with significantly reduced response rates, progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS) in melanoma and NSCLC patients.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Antibiotic-Induced Resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that antibiotics compromise immunotherapy efficacy by inducing gut dysbiosis, reducing microbial diversity, or depleting specific immunogenic bacterial taxa necessary for effective immune priming (e.g., impairing Dendritic Cell or T-cell responses).

content_rubrics.title: Favorable Baseline Microbiome Signatures
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that a baseline gut microbiome enriched with specific beneficial bacteria (e.g., *Ruminococcaceae*, *Prevotella*, *Bifidobacterium*, *Parabacteroides*, or *Bacteroides* species) is correlated with improved responses to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade.

content_rubrics.title: Microbiome-Immune Modulation Mechanisms
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss specific mechanisms by which the microbiome influences anti-tumor immunity, such as the production of metabolites (e.g., Short-Chain Fatty Acids/SCFAs), specific bacterial antigens (e.g., LPS), or the modulation of immune cell infiltration and activation.

content_rubrics.title: Timing of Antibiotic Exposure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the timing of antibiotic administration is critical, with usage shortly before (e.g., within 3 months) or concomitant with immunotherapy initiation being particularly detrimental to outcomes.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that activated p53 upregulates p21 (CDKN1A), which functions to induce cell cycle arrest (G1/S or G2/M) or leads to apoptosis in hPSCs.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that because wild-type p53 induces toxicity in edited cells, the process creates a selection pressure that favors the survival of cells with p53 mutations, posing a risk for genomic instability.

content_rubrics.title: MMEJ and Large Deletions

content_rubrics.title: NHEJ and Small Indels
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ) is typically responsible for small insertions and deletions (indels), contrasting it with the large deletions caused by MMEJ.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that inhibiting components of MMEJ (like POLQ) or NHEJ shifts the DNA repair balance, significantly enhancing Homology-Directed Repair (HDR) efficiency and editing precision.

content_rubrics.title: Predominance of Error-Prone Pathways
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that without intervention, error-prone pathways (NHEJ and MMEJ) generally predominate over HDR in hiPSCs.

content_rubrics.title: Persistence and Expansion Kinetics
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the general consensus that CD28 domains typically drive rapid initial expansion but shorter persistence, whereas 4-1BB domains promote slower expansion but sustained persistence and memory T-cell formation.

content_rubrics.title: Toxicity Profile (CRS & Neurotoxicity)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that CD28-based CAR-T products are generally associated with higher rates and severity of Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity compared to 4-1BB-based products, which tend to have a safer toxicity profile.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that 4-1BB domains may offer advantages in preventing relapse due to reduced exhaustion and better functionality against tumors with low antigen density compared to CD28 domains.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain the biological mechanisms driving these differences, such as 4-1BB activating NF-κB and upregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (e.g., Bcl-xL), versus CD28 driving intense glycolytic metabolism.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical Product Examples
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite specific approved products to illustrate these phenotypes, such as Axicabtagene ciloleucel (CD28) for high expansion/toxicity versus Tisagenlecleucel (4-1BB) for persistence/lower toxicity.

content_rubrics.title: Non-inferior HBV Suppression
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that TAF-based therapy is non-inferior (equivalent) to TDF-based therapy in achieving and maintaining HBV DNA suppression.

content_rubrics.title: Superior Renal Safety Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that TAF is associated with better renal safety outcomes (e.g., less decline in eGFR, fewer markers of proximal tubulopathy) compared to TDF.

content_rubrics.title: Superior Bone Safety Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that TAF is associated with significantly smaller declines in bone mineral density (BMD) compared to TDF.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: Lower Plasma Exposure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that TAF's improved safety profile is attributed to lower plasma/systemic tenofovir concentrations compared to TDF, which reduces tissue toxicity.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of Boosting Agents
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that TAF is particularly advantageous in HIV regimens containing boosters (ritonavir or cobicistat), as these agents increase TDF plasma levels and associated toxicity.

content_rubrics.title: Metabolic Considerations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that while TAF is safer for kidneys/bones, it may be associated with adverse lipid changes (dyslipidemia) or weight gain compared to TDF.

content_rubrics.title: Weak correlation of DNA with rebound timing

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that while reservoir size may not predict the *time* to rebound, it may correlate with the *magnitude* of the viral load upon rebound or the burden in specific cell types.

content_rubrics.title: Significant mortality benefit in ischemic etiology
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that CRT-D is associated with a significant reduction in all-cause mortality compared to CRT-P specifically in patients with ischemic cardiomyopathy.

content_rubrics.title: Inconclusive or lack of mortality benefit in non-ischemic etiology
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that for non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, the difference in all-cause mortality between CRT-D and CRT-P is either not statistically significant or the evidence is less conclusive compared to ischemic cases.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in older patients (e.g., >75 years) or those with significant comorbidities/limited life expectancy, CRT-D offers no survival advantage over CRT-P, particularly in non-ischemic etiologies.

content_rubrics.title: NHEJ Dominance in Quiescent HSPCs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ) is the predominant repair pathway in primary HSPCs, largely because these cells are typically in a quiescent state (G0/G1 phase) where HDR is inactive.

content_rubrics.title: HDR Restriction to S/G2 Phases
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Homology-Directed Repair (HDR) relies on the presence of sister chromatids and is therefore restricted to the S and G2 phases of the cell cycle, making it less efficient in non-cycling HSPCs.

content_rubrics.title: p53 and DDR Activation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that CRISPR-induced double-strand breaks trigger the DNA damage response (DDR) and p53 signaling, which can lead to cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, or reduced HDR efficiency.

content_rubrics.title: MMEJ and POLQ Regulation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss Microhomology-Mediated End Joining (MMEJ) as an error-prone pathway contributing to large deletions and identify DNA Polymerase Theta (POLQ) as a key regulator whose inhibition can favor HDR.

content_rubrics.title: HITI as an NHEJ-based Strategy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify Homology-Independent Targeted Integration (HITI) as a therapeutic strategy that leverages the dominant NHEJ pathway to achieve site-specific integration in quiescent HSPCs, bypassing HDR limitations.

content_rubrics.title: Dosing Frequency Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer must specify that CRISPR-based therapies are designed as a one-time (single-dose) treatment, whereas RNAi therapies require repeated, chronic administration (e.g., every few weeks or months).

content_rubrics.title: Durability of TTR Knockdown
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that CRISPR offers potentially permanent/lifelong durability due to irreversible gene editing, while RNAi effects are reversible and wane if treatment is discontinued.

content_rubrics.title: Magnitude of TTR Reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that both modalities achieve deep serum TTR reductions (generally >80-90%), with CRISPR demonstrating sustained reductions (>90%) that are comparable to or potentially deeper than RNAi.

content_rubrics.title: Distinct Mechanisms of Action
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish the targets: CRISPR edits the DNA (gene) to stop production permanently, whereas RNAi targets the mRNA to inhibit synthesis temporarily.

content_rubrics.title: Maturity of Clinical Evidence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that RNAi therapies have established clinical benefits (e.g., survival, neuropathy scores) from Phase 3 trials, while CRISPR clinical data is currently less mature (e.g., Phase 1, biomarker stabilization).

content_rubrics.title: Absence of Head-to-Head Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly mention that there are currently no direct head-to-head clinical trials comparing in vivo CRISPR editing vs. RNAi therapies.

content_rubrics.title: PARPi vs. ARPI Efficacy in BRCA-mutated
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that in patients with BRCA alterations, PARP inhibitors (alone or in combination) generally provide superior outcomes (e.g., longer PFS) compared to ARPI monotherapy.

content_rubrics.title: General Survival and QoL Benefit
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that NIV is proven to extend survival (e.g., by ~48 days) and improve quality of life in ALS patients with respiratory insufficiency.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of Bulbar Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the survival and QoL benefits of NIV are significantly greater in patients with preserved or normal-to-moderate bulbar function compared to those with severe bulbar dysfunction.

content_rubrics.title: Cochrane Review Citation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the Cochrane Review (Ng et al., 2017) as a key source regarding the efficacy of NIV and the current state of evidence regarding timing.

content_rubrics.title: AAV as Primary Delivery Vector
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors (specifically AAV9 and its variants like AAV-PHP.B/eB) as the most widely studied method for CNS delivery.

content_rubrics.title: Status of Human and Large-Animal Data
content_rubrics.description: The answer should clarify that direct CRISPR CNS delivery data in humans is currently limited or absent, and large-animal (e.g., NHP) data is sparse compared to rodent studies.

content_rubrics.title: Specific NHP Evidence (rAAV9P1)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the study by Bauer et al. (PMID 35398994) involving the synthetic variant rAAV9P1, which demonstrated astrocyte-specific transduction in non-human primates (or at least mention the existence of NHP data for this variant).

content_rubrics.title: Differential Transduction Efficiency
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that while neuronal transduction is often efficient (e.g., >80% with AAV-PHP.B/eB), targeting glia (astrocytes/oligodendrocytes) remains less efficient or requires specific variants.

content_rubrics.title: Delivery Routes (ICV vs IV)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify administration routes used in these studies, such as Intracerebroventricular (ICV) injection or systemic Intravenous (IV) delivery.

content_rubrics.title: Alternative Viral Vectors (HSV)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) vectors as an alternative delivery method explored in preclinical models (referencing Huang et al., PMID 34148538).

content_rubrics.title: NLRP3-Caspase-1-IL-1β Signaling Axis

content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify specific metabolic stressors within the plaque that trigger NLRP3 activation, such as cholesterol crystals, oxidized LDL (oxLDL), or ATP.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain how IL-1β contributes to plaque rupture, specifically mentioning the induction of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) which degrade the extracellular matrix (ECM) and thin the fibrous cap.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that patients with Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS) exhibit heightened inflammasome priming in monocytes or elevated transcoronary cytokine gradients compared to stable patients.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite clinical evidence, specifically the CANTOS trial or the use of canakinumab, demonstrating that targeting the IL-1β pathway reduces recurrent cardiovascular events.

content_rubrics.title: Genetic Loss-of-Function in Mouse Models
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that TREM2 deficiency (knockout) or risk variants (e.g., R47H) in amyloid mouse models (e.g., 5xFAD, LOAD2) lead to exacerbated amyloid pathology, reduced microglial clustering around plaques, and increased synaptic loss or neurite dystrophy.

content_rubrics.title: Human sTREM2 and Synaptic Biomarkers
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that elevated levels of soluble TREM2 (sTREM2) in human cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) correlate with biomarkers of synaptic injury, specifically 14-3-3 proteins (14-3-3ζ/δ), often noted in early disease stages.

content_rubrics.title: Galectin-3 Interaction Mechanism
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that Galectin-3 (Gal3) acts as a ligand for TREM2, driving pro-inflammatory signaling; deleting Gal3 or disrupting this interaction reduces amyloid-associated inflammation and synaptic defects.

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic TREM2 Activation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite evidence that increasing TREM2 signaling via agonists or antibodies (e.g., AL002/AL002c) promotes microglial clustering/plaque compaction and reduces neurite dystrophy.

content_rubrics.title: Microglial Barrier Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the mechanism where TREM2 enables microglia to compact plaques or form a barrier (DAM phenotype), which protects surrounding neurites and synapses from toxicity.

content_rubrics.title: Reduction in Bleeding Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that dual antithrombotic therapy (anticoagulation + single antiplatelet) significantly reduces the risk of major or clinically relevant non-major bleeding compared to triple therapy (anticoagulation + DAPT).

content_rubrics.title: Comparable Composite Efficacy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that dual therapy is generally non-inferior or similar to triple therapy regarding overall composite thrombotic/ischemic endpoints (such as cardiovascular death, stroke, or trial-defined MACE).

content_rubrics.title: Risk of MI or Stent Thrombosis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge a potential trade-off where dual therapy (omitting aspirin) may be associated with a numerically or statistically higher risk of myocardial infarction (MI) or stent thrombosis compared to triple therapy.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference at least one of the major randomized clinical trials defining this standard of care, such as PIONEER AF-PCI, RE-DUAL PCI, AUGUSTUS, or ENTRUST-AF PCI.

content_rubrics.title: Regimen Composition (DOAC + P2Y12)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the preferred dual therapy strategy typically consists of a Direct Oral Anticoagulant (DOAC) combined with a P2Y12 inhibitor (usually clopidogrel).

content_rubrics.title: Non-inferiority of Dolutegravir-based 2DRs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that modern Dolutegravir-based two-drug regimens (specifically DTG + Rilpivirine or DTG + Lamivudine) have demonstrated non-inferiority to standard three-drug regimens in maintaining virologic suppression.

content_rubrics.title: Evidence from SWORD Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the SWORD-1 and SWORD-2 clinical trials, noting that Dolutegravir + Rilpivirine maintained suppression in >95% of participants.

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy of Doravirine + Lamivudine
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention real-world evidence (e.g., Perfezou et al.) indicating that Doravirine + Lamivudine achieves high rates of virologic success (e.g., 98%) with minimal resistance.

content_rubrics.title: Resistance Risk Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that resistance emergence is rare (<1%) in modern INSTI-based 2DRs among patients with no prior virologic failure or resistance mutations.

content_rubrics.title: Real-world Durability and Discontinuation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite recent cohort data (e.g., Bontemps et al., 2025) showing that while durable, a significant portion of patients (approx. 19%) may switch back to 3DRs, often due to non-virologic reasons or adverse events.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison with Historical 2DRs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should contrast modern success with historical data (e.g., Cochrane review) showing that older nucleoside-only 2DRs (like Zidovudine + Lamivudine) were inferior and had higher failure rates.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison of DTG vs. PI-based 2DRs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that Dolutegravir-based 2DRs demonstrate longer durability or lower discontinuation rates compared to Protease Inhibitor (PI)-based 2DRs.

content_rubrics.title: Inpatient Video-EEG as Gold Standard
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that inpatient video-EEG (VEM) is traditionally considered the gold standard for differentiating ES from PNES due to the ability to correlate video with EEG, perform medication withdrawal, and utilize seizure provocation protocols.

content_rubrics.title: Diagnostic Yield of Prolonged Ambulatory EEG
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite recent evidence (specifically Li et al., 2024) indicating that prolonged ambulatory EEG captures diagnostic events in approximately 41-49% of recordings, making it a viable alternative.

content_rubrics.title: Benefit of Extended Monitoring Duration
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the diagnostic yield of ambulatory EEG increases with duration, noting that a significant percentage of events (e.g., ~14%) are captured during extended monitoring periods (e.g., days 5-8) that exceed typical inpatient stays.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical Question Resolution Rate
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that when diagnostic events are captured on ambulatory EEG, the specific clinical question (differentiating ES from PNES) is resolved in a high percentage of cases (approximately 85%).

content_rubrics.title: Inpatient Monitoring Limitations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that inpatient monitoring can result in inconclusive findings (e.g., ~40-42%) if events do not occur spontaneously within the admission timeframe and provocation is unsuccessful.

content_rubrics.title: Methodological Trade-offs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the trade-offs: Ambulatory EEG offers longer monitoring in a natural environment (better for infrequent spontaneous events), while Inpatient VEM offers a controlled environment with real-time observation and safety for interventions.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of Action (Receptor Selectivity)
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that Pimavanserin selectively targets 5-HT2A receptors (inverse agonist/antagonist) and, crucially, does not block Dopamine D2 receptors, unlike most atypical antipsychotics.

content_rubrics.title: Preservation of Motor Function
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that Pimavanserin does not worsen motor symptoms or parkinsonism, distinguishing it from atypical antipsychotics (like clozapine or quetiapine) which may carry risks due to D2 antagonism.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative Efficacy vs. Clozapine
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that while Pimavanserin is effective against placebo, Clozapine is generally considered to have superior or robust efficacy, though it is limited by its safety profile.

content_rubrics.title: Quetiapine Evidence Limitation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that Quetiapine, while widely used off-label, lacks robust evidence of efficacy in clinical trials for PDP.

content_rubrics.title: Mortality and Safety Profile
content_rubrics.description: The answer must mention that Pimavanserin is associated with a lower mortality risk (specifically citing around 35% lower mortality) compared to other atypical antipsychotics.

content_rubrics.title: Monitoring Requirements
content_rubrics.description: The answer should highlight that Pimavanserin does not require the rigorous blood monitoring (for agranulocytosis) that is mandatory for Clozapine.

content_rubrics.description: The answer must describe MET amplification as a frequent EGFR-independent bypass mechanism that sustains tumor growth despite EGFR inhibition.

content_rubrics.title: Histologic Transformation to SCLC
content_rubrics.description: The answer must mention histologic transformation, specifically the transition from NSCLC to Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC), as a mechanism of acquired resistance.

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic Strategy for MET Amplification
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that MET amplification influences treatment selection by prompting the use of MET inhibitors (e.g., capmatinib, tepotinib) in combination with EGFR inhibitors.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should indicate that C797S mutations drive the need for next-generation therapies, such as fourth-generation EGFR inhibitors (or allosteric inhibitors/ADCs).

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic Strategy for Histologic Transformation
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that histologic transformation to SCLC necessitates a switch from EGFR-targeted therapy to standard chemotherapy regimens (e.g., platinum-etoposide).

content_rubrics.title: Activation of Other Bypass Pathways
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the activation of other signaling pathways, such as HER2 amplification, RAS-MAPK, or PI3K-AKT, as drivers of resistance.

content_rubrics.title: Dosing Frequency Comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer must specify that CRISPR-based therapies are designed as a one-time (single-dose) treatment, whereas RNAi therapies require repeated, chronic administration (e.g., every few weeks or months).

content_rubrics.title: Durability of TTR Knockdown
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that CRISPR offers potentially permanent/lifelong durability due to irreversible gene editing, while RNAi effects are reversible and wane if treatment is discontinued.

content_rubrics.title: Magnitude of TTR Reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that both modalities achieve deep serum TTR reductions (generally >80-90%), with CRISPR demonstrating sustained reductions (>90%) that are comparable to or potentially deeper than RNAi.

content_rubrics.title: Distinct Mechanisms of Action
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish the targets: CRISPR edits the DNA (gene) to stop production permanently, whereas RNAi targets the mRNA to inhibit synthesis temporarily.

content_rubrics.title: Maturity of Clinical Evidence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that RNAi therapies have established clinical benefits (e.g., survival, neuropathy scores) from Phase 3 trials, while CRISPR clinical data is currently less mature (e.g., Phase 1, biomarker stabilization).

content_rubrics.title: Absence of Head-to-Head Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly mention that there are currently no direct head-to-head clinical trials comparing in vivo CRISPR editing vs. RNAi therapies.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: RB1 Loss
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify RB1 loss as a primary mechanism of resistance. It should explain that loss of the Retinoblastoma protein removes the substrate for CDK4/6, rendering the inhibitors ineffective as the cell cycle progresses independently of this checkpoint.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: CCNE1 Amplification
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify CCNE1 (Cyclin E1) amplification or overexpression as a key resistance mechanism. It must explain that this drives the cell cycle via CDK2 activation, effectively bypassing the inhibition of CDK4/6.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: ESR1 Mutations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention ESR1 mutations (e.g., Y537S, D538G) as a clinically relevant mechanism of acquired resistance, leading to ligand-independent ER signaling.

content_rubrics.title: Strategy: Targeting CDK2
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss targeting CDK2 (e.g., with specific inhibitors) as a potential strategy to overcome resistance driven by CCNE1 amplification or RB1 loss.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the use of PI3K, AKT, or mTOR inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, everolimus, capivasertib) in combination regimens to target compensatory signaling pathways associated with resistance.

content_rubrics.title: Higher severe CRS rates in ALL vs. Lymphoma
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the incidence of severe CRS (sCRS) is significantly higher in patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) (approx. 35-36%) compared to those with Lymphoma (approx. 13%).

content_rubrics.title: Impact of patient age on CRS severity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that pediatric patients (<18 years) experience higher rates of severe CRS compared to adult populations.

content_rubrics.title: Influence of grading criteria on reported rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that variations in grading systems (e.g., Lee criteria, CTCAE) and specific definitions for hypotension, hypoxia, or organ dysfunction lead to discrepancies in reported CRS rates across studies.

content_rubrics.title: Effect of CAR-T construct (costimulatory domains)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the CAR-T structure influences toxicity, specifically that CD28-based CARs are associated with higher rates of severe CRS compared to 4-1BB-based CARs.

content_rubrics.title: Overall severe CRS incidence statistic
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the overall incidence of severe CRS (grade ≥3) across hematologic malignancies, referencing the meta-analysis finding of approximately 18.5%.

content_rubrics.title: Distinction between all-grade and severe CRS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should distinguish that while all-grade CRS incidence can be high (often >40%), the rate of severe (grade ≥3) CRS is significantly lower.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanistic Distinction (Donor/DSBs)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that HDR relies on double-strand breaks (DSBs) and requires an exogenous donor template, whereas Base Editing (or Prime Editing) corrects mutations without generating DSBs or requiring a donor template.

content_rubrics.title: Efficiency in Post-Mitotic vs. Dividing Cells
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explain that HDR efficiency is significantly lower in post-mitotic (non-dividing) retinal cells due to low homologous recombination activity, whereas Base Editing remains effective in these cells. HDR is noted as effective in proliferating iPSCs.

content_rubrics.title: Specific Base Editing Example (KCNJ13)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the specific example of Adenine Base Editors (e.g., ABE8e) correcting the *KCNJ13* mutation in patient-derived iPSC-RPE cells (or similar specific in vitro evidence).

content_rubrics.title: Specific HDR Application in iPSCs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that HDR is successfully used to correct mutations (e.g., *ABCA4*, *OPA1*, *NR2E3*) in patient-derived iPSCs (proliferating stage) to generate isogenic lines.

content_rubrics.title: Safety Profile (Indels/Off-targets)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that Base Editing generally offers a safer profile with reduced risks of indel formation or off-target effects compared to HDR, as it avoids double-strand breaks.

content_rubrics.title: Association with Increased Mortality
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that delayed administration of appropriate antibiotic therapy is generally identified as a significant risk factor for increased mortality in patients with sepsis caused by ESBL-producing Enterobacterales.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of Severity and Comorbidities
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the prognostic outcome is heavily influenced by the severity of illness (e.g., septic shock, ICU admission, high Pitt bacteremia score) and patient comorbidities, which can be independent predictors of mortality alongside or confounding the delay in therapy.

content_rubrics.title: Empiric vs. Definitive Therapy Nuance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss the nuance that inappropriate *empirical* therapy may not always result in higher mortality if appropriate *definitive* therapy is administered once susceptibility results are known (referencing findings like Kang et al., 2004, or mixed evidence).

content_rubrics.title: ESBL Status and Treatment Delay Link
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that ESBL-producing infections are associated with a higher frequency of inappropriate initial therapy compared to non-ESBL infections, which contributes to the observed mortality differences.

content_rubrics.title: Quantification of Risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide quantitative evidence (e.g., Odds Ratios, Relative Risks, or percentage comparisons) from studies to illustrate the magnitude of the mortality risk associated with delayed therapy or ESBL status.

content_rubrics.description: The answer references the ATLAS trial or the concept of treating presymptomatic SOD1 mutation carriers (identified by rising NfL) to delay disease onset compared to natural history.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify that a longer duration of epilepsy (or delay to surgery) is a significant predictor of poorer surgical outcomes and higher risk of seizure recurrence.

content_rubrics.title: History of Febrile Seizures
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that a history of febrile seizures is associated with better postoperative seizure control (often linked to HS).

content_rubrics.title: Lack of Quantitative Frequency Data
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that the specific frequency or prevalence of protocol-mandated exclusions due to PK DDIs in Phase III oncology trials is not explicitly quantified in the retrieved literature.

content_rubrics.title: Persistence of Early-Phase Criteria
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that exclusion criteria regarding strong/moderate enzyme inhibitors or inducers often originate in early-phase trials to ensure safety and accurate dose determination, and these criteria may persist into Phase III trials by default.

content_rubrics.title: Impact on Generalizability
content_rubrics.description: The answer must discuss the implications for generalizability, specifically that excluding patients on common medications (like anticonvulsants or antifungals) reduces the trial's applicability to the real-world patient population.
